mnemonics.title: The 9 Principles Phrase
mnemonics.content: **L**azy **D**evs **F**all **S**hort **E**xpecting **O**ptimal **L**ayered **P**rotection **C**ode. (Stands for: Least Privilege, Defense in Depth, Fail Secure, Separation of Duties, Economy of Mechanism, Open Design, Least Common Mechanism, Psychological Acceptability, Complete Mediation).

mnemonics.title: Open Design Logic
mnemonics.content: Security should rely on the **Key** (Password/Secret), not the **Lock** (Algorithm/Design). Even if attackers see the code (Open Design), they cannot get in without the key.

mnemonics.title: Fail Secure Behavior
mnemonics.content: Think of a bank vault. If the electronic system fails, it defaults to LOCKED (Secure), not OPEN. Security systems must fail to a state that denies access (Secure).

acronyms.usage: The fundamental goals of information security. All design principles aim to protect these three pillars.
The core functions of access control. Used to implement principles like Least Privilege and Separation of Duties.
The three main threats (breaches, malware, deletion). Design principles act as countermeasures to these threats.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine an Onion or a Castle. You must get past the moat (network perimeter), the walls (firewall), the guards (intrusion detection), and the inner keep (data encryption) to reach the core.
Think of a restricted area in an airport. You only get a boarding pass for your specific flight (specific resource), not access to the tarmac or the cockpit (full system).
Two keys required to launch a nuclear missile. One person cannot complete a high-risk transaction alone; they need a second person to authorize it.
Compare a simple, sturdy padlock to a complex mechanical locking mechanism with 50 gears. Simple systems are easier to audit, test, and less likely to fail.

analogies.analogy: The recipe for Coca-Cola. The formula is secret (the key), but the recipe can be published (open design). Security lies in the secrecy of the key, not the obscurity of the design.

Password policies. If a system forces you to change your password every hour with impossible complexity, users will write it on a sticky note, creating a bigger vulnerability than a reasonable policy.
Virtual Machines (VMs). If one VM is hacked, it doesn't necessarily crash the host because they share very little 'mechanism' and are isolated from each other.
Hotel Key Cards. Every time you enter a room, the system checks your card. It does not assume that because you entered once, you are authorized to enter again later.
